mappings.rationaleAI: Contemporary India's economic transformation, caste dynamics, democratic governance, and emergence as a global power directly address India since 1947 (A.2), domestic affairs under Nehru and Congress Party (A.2.a), and foreign policy including nonalignment and disputes with Pakistan (A.2.b).

Gandhi's satyagraha movement and leadership of Indian independence directly address intensified agitation for independence and Gandhi's nonviolent resistance (A.1.a), including the Round Table Conference and Government of India Act.

Pakistan as a postcolonial nation-state created in 1947 from British India, partition-era tensions, and governance challenges directly address the movement for a separate Muslim state and partition (A.1.b) and Pakistan since 1947 (A.3).

Southeast Asia's eleven nations covering Myanmar, Thailand, Malaysia, Vietnam, Philippines, Laos, Cambodia, and Indonesia from colonial era to the present directly address mainland Southeast Asia since 1920 (B) and Indonesia and the Philippines (C).
Hindu nationalism, the Indian National Congress in shaping Hindu identity, and caste connect to the independence movement (A.1.a) and postwar Indian domestic affairs (A.2).
Islam's global scope and adaptation to modernity connect to the increased strength of the Muslim League and movement for Pakistan (A.1.b), Pakistan since 1947 (A.3), and Islam in Indonesia (C.1).
Theravada Buddhism across South and Southeast Asia connects to Ceylon/Sri Lanka (A.5), Tibetan Buddhism and the Dalai Lama (A.6), and Theravada's role in Thailand and mainland Southeast Asia (B.3).
The Dalai Lama institution, Chinese suppression, and Tibetan Buddhist development directly address Tibet since 1920 including Chinese invasion and government control (A.6).
Sikh history in Punjab, the diaspora, and religious identity connect to the partition of British India and the emergence of Pakistan and India as separate states (A.1.b).

British imperial administration and decolonization connect to India's path to independence (A.1), British Ceylon (A.5), British rule in Burma and Malaya (B.1), and British involvement in Malaya (B.2).

British, Dutch, and French colonial empires connect to India under British imperialism (A.1), European colonial presence in mainland Southeast Asia (B), and Dutch and other colonial powers in Indonesia and the Philippines (C).

Anti-colonial activism and independence movements, including Nehru as a leader, directly address Indian independence and partition (A.1, A.1.b), emergence of independent Southeast Asian states (B), and Indonesian and Philippine independence (C).

Postcolonial experiences of colonized societies in Asia connect to the entire section's coverage of the late colonial period and emergence of new nations across South Asia (A), mainland Southeast Asia (B), and Indonesia and the Philippines (C).

Nationalism and self-determination, including the Kashmir case, connect to Indian independence agitation (A.1.a), emergence of Burmese nationalism (B.1.a), and Dutch suppression of Indonesian nationalist revolts (C.1.a.i).

Ho Chi Minh's Vietnamese revolution and communism in Southeast Asia address the Indochina Communist Party (B.4), Ho Chi Minh's government and wars in Vietnam (B.4.b, B.4.c), and Indonesian communist suppression (C.1.b).
Cold War proxy wars and the Vietnam War address U.S. military intervention in Vietnam (B.4.c) and Cold War dynamics in Indonesia's anti-communist purge (C.1.b).
Intrastate armed conflicts connect to Sri Lanka's Tamil insurgency and protracted civil war (A.5.b), the Vietnam War (B.4.c), and Cambodia's brutal internal conflicts (B.4.f).
Modern warfare including guerrilla tactics and the Cold War connects to the Indochina Wars, Vietnam War, and U.S. military intervention (B.4.c).

Japan's expansion in Southeast Asia and the Pacific addresses political and economic effects of World War II on India (A.1.b), Japanese occupation of Burma (B.1.a), Malaya (B.2.a), Thailand (B.3.a), Indonesia (C.1.a.i), and the Philippines (C.2.a).
Concentration camps under the Khmer Rouge directly address the brutal collectivization, resettlement, and large-scale deaths in Cambodia under Democratic Kampuchea (B.4.f).
Democracy and democratic transitions connect to India's parliamentary system under Nehru (A.2.a) and Indonesia's post-Suharto transition to democracy (C.1.b).
Authoritarian military regimes connect to Myanmar's military government under Ne Win (B.1.b) and Suharto's administration in Indonesia (C.1.b).
Human rights and crimes against humanity connect to the Khmer Rouge's mass atrocities in Cambodia (B.4.f) and massive depopulation of East Timor through military action (C.1.b).
Forced migration and refugee crises connect to the exodus of ethnic Chinese refugees from Vietnam (B.4.d) and border-crossing effects of Southeast Asian conflicts.
International migration connects to Chinese immigration in Malaya (B.2.a) and the exodus of ethnic Chinese from Vietnam (B.4.d).
Diplomatic history connects to India's foreign policy including nonalignment, disputes with Pakistan over Kashmir, and border conflict with China (A.2.b).
Nuclear deterrence and Cold War dynamics provide context for U.S. military intervention in Vietnam (B.4.c) within the broader Cold War strategic framework.
Geopolitics and strategic geographic factors connect to India's border conflicts with Pakistan and China (A.2.b) and the strategic dimensions of the Indochina War (B.4.c).

Indian cinema addressing caste discrimination, gender oppression, and national identity under postcolonial conditions connects to India's domestic political, social, and cultural developments since 1947 (A.2).
